mainmatter.p: Let A be a contractible type with centre a. For any family of types B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type}, we may define a function \mathsf {ind}_{ A,a } \colon B a \to \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x : A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x : A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x : A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x : A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx satisfying \mathsf {ind}_{ A,a } \, b \, a = b.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Let \chi _x \colon a = x be the witness of contraction for any x:A. Given b : Ba and x:A, we define \mathsf {ind}_{ A,a } \, b \, x : Bx by transporting b along the identification \chi _a ^{-1} \cdot \chi _x \colon a = x:
Then we compute \mathsf {ind}_{ A,a } \, b \, x \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( \chi _a ^{-1} \cdot \chi _a \right ) \mathclose {} _* b = \mathsf {refl} _*b = b.

frontmatter.title: Path objects and the univalence axiom

mainmatter.p: A reflexive graph is given by a type A together with a family of types \mathopen {} \left ( \cong _A \right ) \mathclose {} \colon A \to A \to \mathsf {Type} and an assignment 1_{ x } : x \cong x to each x,y: A. The reflexive graph is called univalent when for all x:A the type \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } x \cong _A y is a proposition, and so is contractible with centre \mathopen {} \left ( x, 1_{ x } \right ) \mathclose {}.
We shall refer to a univalent reflexive graph as a path object.
We will write \mathsf {PathObj} for the (large) type of path objects. In our notation, we will at times silently coerce a given path object to its underlying type of vertices.
The intrinsic path object on a type A, written A ^{=}, is defined by setting x \cong _{ A ^{=} } y : \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( x = y \right ) \mathclose {} and 1_{ x } : \equiv \mathsf {refl}.
mainmatter.block: Proof. That the reflexive graph defined above is univalent follows immediately by identification induction.

mainmatter.p: The reflexive graph structure on types given by equivalences is univalent. We shall write \mathsf {Type} to indicate the resulting (large) path object of types and equivalences.

frontmatter.title: The univalence principle for dependent functions

mainmatter.p: The reflexive graph structure on \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx} by homotopy and identity homotopies is univalent.

Given a dependent function f \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}, if each component Bx is contractible with centre fx then the dependent product \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx} is contractible with centre f.
mainmatter.block: Proof. This follows immediately from univalence for functions.

mainmatter.p: The dependent product of a family of propositions is a proposition.
mainmatter.block: Proof. Let B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type} be a family of propositions; fixing f \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}, it suffices to show any other g \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx may be identified with f. As B is a family of propositions, we know that each Bx is contractible with centre fx. Therefore, the closure of contractible types under dependent products () shows that \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx is contractible with centre f.

mainmatter.p: Let B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} be a family of path objects. We equip the dependent product \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx} with the structure of a path object, setting f \cong _{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}} g : \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } fx \cong _{Bx}gx and 1_{ f } : \equiv \lambda x \mathpunct {.} 1_{ fx }.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Univalence follows from the closure of families of contractible types under dependent products () via the distributivity of \prod over \sum.

mainmatter.p: If f \colon A \to B has a quasi-inverse g \colon B \to A, then a path object structure on A induces a path object structure on B, setting \mathopen {} \left ( x \cong _B y \right ) \mathclose {} : \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( gx \cong _A gy \right ) \mathclose {}.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We first prove the lemma for biinvertible functions, so that both the section and retraction are tracked by g. In this case, we may use singleton induction via the univalence principle for types to restrict attention to the case that f is the identity map, and the result is immediate.

mainmatter.p: Let A be a path object, and let B \colon A \to \mathsf {Prop} be a family of propositions. Then the dependent sum projection \pi _1 \colon \mathopen {} \left ( \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx \right ) \mathclose {} \to A creates a path object structure \mathopen {} \left \{ x:A \, \middle \vert \, Bx \right \} \mathclose {} on \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx as follows:

ol.li: we have \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x:A \, \middle \vert \, Bx \right \} \mathclose {} } \mathopen {} \left ( y,v \right ) \mathclose {} definitionally equal to x \cong _A y;
the reflexivity datum 1_{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} } is given by 1_{ x } in A;
univalence follows from univalence of A and the fact that B is valued in propositions.

mainmatter.p: Let H \colon f \sim g be a homotopy between two functions f,g \colon A \to B. For any identification p \colon x =y in A, we have an identification H x \cdot \mathsf {ap}_{ g } \, p = \mathsf {ap}_{ f } \, p \cdot Hy.
mainmatter.block: Proof. By the univalence principle for functions (), we may consider just the case that f \equiv g and H \equiv 1_{ f }, and so we must show that \mathsf {refl} \cdot \mathsf {ap}_{ g } p = \mathsf {ap}_{ f } p \cdot \mathsf {refl}. By identification induction, we may assume x \equiv y and p \equiv \mathsf {refl} to show \mathsf {refl} \cdot \mathsf {refl} = \mathsf {refl} \cdot \mathsf {refl}, which is immediate.

frontmatter.title: Coherence for homotopies onto the identity

mainmatter.p: Let H \colon f \sim 1_{ A } be a homotopy from f \colon A \to A onto the identity function. Then we may construct a higher homotopy from H \circ f to \mathsf {ap}_{ f } \circ H.
mainmatter.block: Proof. By the univalence principle for functions (), we may consider only the case that f \equiv 1_{ A } and H \equiv 1_{ 1_{ A } } exhibit the identity homotopy 1_{ 1_{ 1_{ A } } } \colon 1_{ 1_{ A } } \sim 1_{ 1_{ A } }.

mainmatter.p: We have seen in how to obtain principles for equational reasoning by imposing path object structures on types; in general, we will need an analogous tool for working with families of types B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type} given a path object A. Such a tool must account for heterogeneous identifications in B, i.e. identifications between elements of different fibers of B; in this section, we explore three possible variations on the concept of “dependent path object”:

ol.li: displayed path objects provide an unbiased and heterogeneous account of identifications between elements of different fibers of B;
cocartesian families of path objects reduce heterogeneous identification to homogeneous identification by means of a forward transport operation;
cartesian families of path objects reduce heterogeneous identification to homogeneous identification by means of a backward transport operation.

mainmatter.p: All of these notions are useful: because it is unbiased (i.e. prefers neither forward nor backward transport), the displayed representation is the most fundamental; on the other hand, the biased cartesian and cocartesian representations are the most frequently used. The fact that the latter are described directly for families of path objects in the existing sense is practically important for the compositionality of reasoning in univalent foundations, as we will more frequently have general-purpose building blocks for composing path objects than for composing displayed path objects.

Let A be a path object. A displayed reflexive graph over A is given by families of types B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type} and \mathopen {} \left ( \cong _B^ \bullet \right ) \mathclose {} \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x,y:A \right \} \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x,y:A \right \} \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x,y:A \right \} \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x,y:A \right \} \mathclose {} } } x \cong _A y \to Bx \to By \to \mathsf {Type} together with an assignment of displayed reflexivity data 1_{ u } \colon b \cong _B^{ 1_{ x } } b for every u:Bx. The displayed reflexive graph is called univalent when for each x:A the induced reflexive graph \mathopen {} \left ( \cong _{Bx} \right ) \mathclose {} : \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( \cong ^{ 1_{ x } }_B \right ) \mathclose {} on Bx is univalent. We define a displayed path object to be a univalent displayed reflexive graph.

We will write \mathsf {DispPathObj} \, A for the (large) type of displayed path objects over A. We will at times silently coerce a displayed path object B \colon \mathsf {DispPathObj} \, A to its underlying family of vertices B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type}.
Let A be a path object; an A-indexed family B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} of path objects is called a cocartesian family when it comes equipped with an opcleaving as defined below:

ol.li: a pushforward operation sending each p \colon x \cong _A y to some specified map p_* \colon Bx \to By;

a family \Phi _B \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left ( 1_{ x } \right ) \mathclose {} _*b \cong _{Bx} b witnessing the unit law that pushforward along the identity element 1_{ x } \colon x \cong _A x is the identity function.

mainmatter.p: Let A be a path object; an A-indexed family B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} of path objects is called fibrant when it comes equipped with a cleaving as defined below:

ol.li: a restriction operation sending each p \colon x \cong _A y to some specified map p^* \colon By \to Bx;

a family \Phi _B \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } } b \cong _{Bx} 1_{ x } ^*b witnessing the unit law that restriction along the identity element 1_{ x } \colon x \cong _A x is the identity function.

mainmatter.p: Our goal is to show that cartesian and cocartesian families of path objects both give rise to appropriate displayed path objects on their base. We will do this incrementally.

frontmatter.title: Display of cocartesian families of path objects

mainmatter.p: Let A be a path object. Any cocartesian family of path objects B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} gives rise to a canonical displayed path object over A in which:

ol.li: for p \colon x \cong _A y, we define b \cong _B^p b' to be p_*b \cong _{By} b';

for x:A and b:Bx, the identity element 1_{ b } \colon b \cong _B^{ 1_{ x } }b is given by the component \Phi _Bxb \colon \mathopen {} \left ( 1_{ x } \right ) \mathclose {} _*b \cong _{Bx}b of the unit law of B.

mainmatter.block: Proof. Univalence is easily established using the univalence of each Bx

frontmatter.title: The total path object of a displayed path object

mainmatter.p: Let A be a path object, and let B: \mathsf {DispPathObj} \, A be a displayed path object structure over A. The total path object of B over A, written A.B, is defined as follows:

Reflexivity data is given by assigning 1_{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} } : \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( 1_{ x } , 1_{ u } \right ) \mathclose {}. Univalence is established in two singleton inductions, first using univalence of \mathopen {} \left ( \cong _A \right ) \mathclose {} followed by univalence of \mathopen {} \left ( \cong _B^ \bullet \right ) \mathclose {}.

frontmatter.title: Covariant sum of an cocartesian family of path objects

mainmatter.p: Let B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} be an cocartesian family of path objects over a path object A. We define the covariant sum of B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj}, written \mathchoice { \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}, to be the total path object of the displayed path object associated to B. In particular, we have defined \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}} \mathopen {} \left ( y,v \right ) \mathclose {} to be \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } } p_*u \cong _{By} v.

frontmatter.title: The univalence principle for reflexive graphs

mainmatter.p: We may define a path object structure on the type of reflexive graphs with vertices in a given type A, so that an isomorphism from \mathopen {} \left ( G, 1_{ \bullet } \right ) \mathclose {} to \mathopen {} \left ( G', 1_{ \bullet } ' \right ) \mathclose {} is given by a pair of a pointwise equivalence of types H \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Gxy \cong G'xy that preserves reflexivity data as witnessed by a dependent homotopy I \colon \mathopen {} \left ( \lambda x \mathpunct {.} H \, x \, x \, 1_{ x } \right ) \mathclose {} \sim 1_{ \bullet } '.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We first show that the dependent product of intrinsic path objects \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left ( -xx \right ) \mathclose {} ^{=} is a cocartesian family of path objects over the path object \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type}: given an equivalence H : G \cong G' of graphs and a reflexivity datum 1_{ \bullet } \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Gxx, we define H_* 1_{ \bullet } x : \equiv H \, x \, x \, 1_{ x }; the unit law holds definitionally.

Therefore, we may form the covariant sum \mathchoice { \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( G: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( G: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( G: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( G: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type} \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left ( Gxx \right ) \mathclose {} ^{=}, in which equivalences are given by homotopies of reflexivity data over graph equivalences.

frontmatter.title: Display of cartesian families of path objects

mainmatter.p: Let A be a path object. Any cartesian family of path objects B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} gives rise to a canonical displayed path object over A in which:

ol.li: for p \colon x \cong _A y, we define b \cong _B^p b' to be b \cong _{By} p^*b';
for x:A and b:Bx, the identity element 1_{ b } \colon b \cong _B^{ 1_{ x } }b is given by the component \Phi _Bxb \colon b \cong _{Bx} 1_{ x } ^*b of the unit law of B.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Univalence is more difficult to establish than for the case of cocartesian families (). Fixing a:A, we must show that the reflexive graph structure on B a with edges \mathopen {} \left ( b \cong _B^{ 1_{ a } } b' \right ) \mathclose {} \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( b \cong _{Ba} 1_{ a } ^*b \right ) \mathclose {} and reflexivity elements for b:Ba given by \Phi _B ab \colon b \cong _{Ba} 1_{ a } ^*b is univalent.

We proceed by identifying this reflexive graph structure with the assumed one on Ba using the univalence principle for reflexive graphs (), and using the fact that the latter is assumed to be univalent. The desired equivalence of reflexive graphs is given by straightforward path algebra.

frontmatter.title: Contravariant sum of cartesian families of path objects

mainmatter.p: Let B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} be an cartesian family of path objects over a path object A. We define the contravariant sum of B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj}, written \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}, to be the total path object of the displayed path object associated to B as in . In particular, we have defined \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx} \mathopen {} \left ( y,v \right ) \mathclose {} to be \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } } u \cong _{Bx} p^*v.

frontmatter.title: The intrinsic opcleaving of a family of path objects

mainmatter.p: Given a type A and a family of path objects B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj}, we can always define a opcleaving rendering B fibrant over the intrinsic path object on A. The opcleaving is obtained directly from the transport operation of the identification type.

Given f \colon A \to B and b:B, we shall impose a path object structure on the fiber \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } fx=b of f over b. The family \mathopen {} \left ( f-=b \right ) \mathclose {} ^{=} \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} given by intrinsic path objects in each component is a cartesian family over A ^{=}:

ol.li: for p \colon x = y and \phi :fy=b, we define p^*b \colon fx=b to be identification \mathsf {ap}_{ f } p \cdot \phi;
the restriction counit law can be seen to hold using the unit laws for composition of identification.

mainmatter.p: Therefore, we may compute the contravariant sum \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } } { \mathopen {} \left ( fx=b \right ) \mathclose {} ^{=} } using the cleaving above, with \mathopen {} \left ( x, \phi \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } } { \mathopen {} \left ( fx=b \right ) \mathclose {} ^{=} }} \mathopen {} \left ( y, \psi \right ) \mathclose {} definitionally equal to \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p \colon x= y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p \colon x= y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p \colon x= y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p \colon x= y \right ) \mathclose {} } } \phi = \mathsf {ap}_{ f } p \cdot \psi.

frontmatter.title: The standard cleaving for sectionhood witnesses

mainmatter.p: Let f \colon A \to B be a given function. Given a homotopy H \colon s \sim s' \colon B \to A and a witness h \colon f \circ s' \sim 1_{ B } that s' is a section of f, we may construct a witness H^*h \colon f \circ s \sim 1_{ B } that s is a section of f as follows:
mainmatter.tex: H^*h x : \equiv \mathsf {ap}_{ f } \mathopen {} \left ( Hx \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot h x
mainmatter.p: As we evidently have 1_{ s } ^*h x = \mathsf {ap}_{ f } \mathsf {refl} \cdot h x = h x, the data above constitute a cleaving of f \circ- \sim 1_{ B } over the path object B \to A ^{=}, both with their standard path object structure as given by (dependent) homotopies in . In this sense, sectionhood witnesses constitute a cartesian family of path objects.

frontmatter.title: The standard cleaving for retractionhood witnesses

mainmatter.p: Let f \colon A \to B be a given function. Given a homotopy H \colon r \sim r' \colon B \to A and a witness h \colon r' \circ f \sim 1_{ A } that r' is a retraction of f, we may construct a witness H^*h \colon r \circ f \sim 1_{ A } that r is a retraction of f as follows:
mainmatter.tex: H^*h x : \equiv H \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot h x
mainmatter.p: As we have 1_{ r } ^*h x = h x, the data above constitute a cleaving of - \circ f \sim 1_{ B } over B \to A ^{=}, both with their standard path object structure as given by (dependent) homotopies in . Thus retractionhood witnesses constitute a cartesian family of path objects.

A suitable path object on sections to a fixed function r \colon A \to B is given by a contravariant sum of path objects \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( s:B \to A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( s:B \to A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( s:B \to A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( s:B \to A ^{=} \right ) \mathclose {} } } r \circ s \sim 1_{ B }, as sectionhood witnesses form a cartesian family over their underlying sections (). In particular, an isomorphism of sections of r from \mathopen {} \left ( s,h \right ) \mathclose {} to \mathopen {} \left ( s',h' \right ) \mathclose {} is given by a homotopy H_s \colon s \sim s' together with a higher homotopy H_h \colon h \sim \mathopen {} \left ( \lambda x \mathpunct {.} \mathsf {ap}_{ r } \mathopen {} \left ( H_s x \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot h'x \right ) \mathclose {}.

A suitable path object on retractions to a fixed function s \colon B \to A is given by a contravariant sum of path objects \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( r:A \to B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( r:A \to B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( r:A \to B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( r:A \to B \right ) \mathclose {} } } r \circ s \sim 1_{ B }, as retractionhood witnesses form a cartesian family over their underlying sections (). In particular, an isomorphism of retractions of s from \mathopen {} \left ( r,h \right ) \mathclose {} to \mathopen {} \left ( r',h' \right ) \mathclose {} is given by a homotopy H_r \colon r \sim r' together with a higher homotopy H_h \colon h \sim \mathopen {} \left ( \lambda x \mathpunct {.} H_r \mathopen {} \left ( r x \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot h'x \right ) \mathclose {}.

frontmatter.title: Further closure properties of truncation levels

mainmatter.p: If A is a proposition and B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type} is a proposition, then the dependent sum \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx} is a proposition.
mainmatter.block: Proof. We fix pairs \mathopen {} \left ( a,b \right ) \mathclose {} and \mathopen {} \left ( a',b' \right ) \mathclose {} in \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}. By means of the total path object on the pointwise displayed path object on B, it suffices to exhibit identifications p \colon a = a' and p_* b = b'. These are given respectively by our assumptions that A and Ba' are propositions.

mainmatter.p: It is a proposition that a given type A is contractible.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Suppose we have two centres of contraction a,a':A with \chi _ \bullet \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } a = x and \chi '_ \bullet \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } a'=x as witnesses of contraction. To identify \mathopen {} \left ( a, \chi _ \bullet \right ) \mathclose {} with \mathopen {} \left ( a', \chi '_ \bullet \right ) \mathclose {}, we employ the covariant sum of each of the pointwise path object structures on \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } -=x over the intrinsic path object structure on A, constructing an identification p \colon a = a' followed by a homotopy H \colon \mathopen {} \left ( \mathopen {} \left ( \chi _{a'} \right ) \mathclose {} _*^{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } -=x} \chi _ \bullet \right ) \mathclose {} \sim \chi '_ \bullet. Fixing z \colon A, we compute:

The first and second steps follow from general computation lemmas for transport, and the last step follows from the fact that A is a set, which we may deduce as contractible types are propositions and propositions are sets.

frontmatter.title: Being a proposition is a proposition

mainmatter.p: It is a proposition that a given type A is a proposition.
mainmatter.block: Proof. To identify two witnesses \phi , \phi ' \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } x = y that A is a proposition, it suffices via the pointwise univalent univalence principle for functions to exhibit a double homotopy in A identifying each \phi xy with \phi 'xy. As propositions are sets, this follows from our assumption that A is a proposition.

mainmatter.p: It is a proposition that a given type A is n-truncated.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We proceed by induction on the truncation level n.

ol.li: When n=-2, we recall that being contractible is a proposition.
When n=-1, we recall that being a proposition is a proposition.

For n \geq 0, we assume by induction that being n-1-truncated is a proposition to prove that being n-truncated is a proposition. In other words, we must show that \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {is-truncated} \, \mathopen {} \left ( n-1 \right ) \mathclose {} \, A is a proposition. By our inductive hypothesis, this is a dependent product of propositions and it is a proposition.

frontmatter.title: Being an n-truncated map is a proposition

mainmatter.p: That a given map f \colon A \to B is n-truncated is a proposition.
mainmatter.block: Proof. We must check that the product \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:B \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {is-truncated} \, \mathsf {fiber} \, f \, x is a proposition; indeed, this is a product of propositions as being an n-truncated type is a proposition.

frontmatter.title: Being a surjection is a proposition

mainmatter.p: That a given map f \colon E \to B is surjective is a proposition.
mainmatter.block: Proof. Because the dependent product of a family of propositions is a proposition.

mainmatter.p: The dependent product of a family of propositions is a proposition.
mainmatter.block: Proof. Let B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type} be a family of propositions; fixing f \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}, it suffices to show any other g \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx may be identified with f. As B is a family of propositions, we know that each Bx is contractible with centre fx. Therefore, the closure of contractible types under dependent products () shows that \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx is contractible with centre f.

mainmatter.p: Let f \colon A \to B have a quasi-inverse g \colon B \to A with incoherent counit \epsilon \colon f \circ g \sim 1_{ B } and unit \eta \colon g \circ f \sim 1_{ A }. We can correct the counit to obtain a coherent inverse to f \colon A \to B.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: The corrected counit \epsilon ' \colon g \circ f \sim 1_{ A } is defined like so:
block.tex: \epsilon ' x : \equiv \epsilon \mathopen {} \left ( f \mathopen {} \left ( f ^{-1} x \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} ^{-1} \cdot \mathsf {ap}_{ f } \mathopen {} \left ( \eta \mathopen {} \left ( f ^{-1} x \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot \epsilon x
block.p: The coherence \chi \colon \mathsf {ap}_{ f } \circ \eta \sim \epsilon ' \circ f can be constructed using the naturality of homotopies and the coherence of homotopies onto the identity.

mainmatter.p: Let f \colon A \to B be biinvertible with s,r \colon B \to A and h_s \colon f \circ s \sim 1_{ B } and h_r \colon r \circ f = 1_{ A }. Then we may define a homotopy h' \colon s \circ f \sim 1_{ A } exhibiting s \colon B \to A as a retraction of f \colon A \to B. Therefore, we may define a quasi-inverse to f tracked by s \colon B \to A.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We have s \circ f \sim r \circ f \circ s \circ f by h_r and r \circ f \circ s \circ f \sim r \circ f by h_s and r \circ f \sim 1_{ A } by h_r.

mainmatter.p: Any invertible map f \colon A \to B is contractible.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Let \epsilon \colon f \circ f ^{-1} = 1_{ B } be the counit of the inverse to f \colon A \to B. Given b:B, we must show that the fiber of f over b is contractible; the centre of contraction is given by the pair \mathopen {} \left ( f ^{-1} x, \epsilon x \right ) \mathclose {}, and the required paths can be constructed via the univalence principle for fibers using the unit \eta \colon f ^{-1} \circ f = 1_{ A }, the coherence \chi \colon \mathsf {ap}_{ f } \circ \eta \sim \epsilon \circ f, and the naturality of homotopies.

mainmatter.p: Any contractible map f \colon A \to B is a section.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Let f \colon A \to B be contractible; we define a retraction r \colon B \to A to take b:B to the first component of the centre of contraction of the fiber of f over b. To construct h_r \colon r \circ f \sim 1_{ A } exhibiting r as a retraction of f, we use the identification between any two elements of the fiber of f over fx.

mainmatter.p: Any contractible map f \colon A \to B is a retraction.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Let f \colon A \to B be contractible; we define a section s \colon B \to A to take b:B to the first component of the centre of contraction of the fiber of f over b. The witness h_s \colon f \circ s \sim 1_{ B } exhibiting s as a section of f, we use the second component of the centre of contraction.

mainmatter.p: Any contractible map f \colon A \to B is a biinvertible.
mainmatter.block: Proof. Because contractible maps are both sections and retractions.

mainmatter.p: Any quasi-invertible map f \colon A \to B is contractible.
mainmatter.block: Proof. Any quasi-inverse can be improved to a coherent inverse, and any coherently invertible map is contractible.

mainmatter.p: Any biinvertible map f \colon A \to B is contractible.
mainmatter.block: Proof. As biinvertible maps are quasi-invertible and quasi-invertible maps are contractible.

mainmatter.p: Let f \colon A \to B be biinvertible; then for any type C, the postcomposition map \mathopen {} \left ( f \circ - \right ) \mathclose {} \colon \mathopen {} \left ( C \to A \right ) \mathclose {} \to \mathopen {} \left ( C \to B \right ) \mathclose {} is biinvertible.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: The section and retraction are built using by postcomposing with the designated section and retraction of f; the witnesses for the postcomposition sections and retractions are constructed using the univalence principle for function spaces.

mainmatter.p: Let f \colon A \to B be biinvertible; then for any type C, the precomposition map \mathopen {} \left ( - \circ f \right ) \mathclose {} \colon \mathopen {} \left ( B \to C \right ) \mathclose {} \to \mathopen {} \left ( A \to C \right ) \mathclose {} is biinvertible.
mainmatter.block: Proof. Analogous to the corresponding result for pushforward along biinvertible maps.

mainmatter.p: Let f \colon A \to B be a section of some r \colon B \to A; the type of sections of f is, then, a proposition.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Using the univalence principle for dependent function spaces, we can see that the type of sections of f \colon A \to B is a retract of the fiber of f \circ - at 1_{ B }, using the univalence principle for sections.

To show that the type of sections of f is a proposition, we may assume beforehand that we have such a section of f; therefore, because we have assumed a retraction of f already, we know that f is biinvertible and therefore that f is contractible. Because propositions are closed under retracts, it suffices to show that the fiber of f \circ - at 1_{ B } is a proposition, which follows because f is contractible.

mainmatter.p: Let f \colon A \to B be a retraction of some s \colon B \to A; the type of retractions of f is, then, a proposition.
mainmatter.block: Proof. Analogous to the corresponding result for sections of sections, using the fact that the type of retractions of f can be seen to be a retract of the fiber of - \circ f at 1_{ A }.

mainmatter.p: The type of biinverses of a given function f \colon A \to B is a proposition.
mainmatter.block: Proof. Sections of sections and retractions of retractions are unique.

mainmatter.p: Any biinvertible map f \colon A \to B is an embedding.
mainmatter.block: Proof. We must show that each fiber of f is a proposition; in fact, each fiber is contractible as biinvertible maps are contractible, and contractible types are propositions.

mainmatter.p: Any biinvertible map f \colon A \to B is surjective.
mainmatter.block: Proof. This is immediate, using the unit map of propositional truncation and the section of f.

mainmatter.p: A function f \colon A \to B that is both surjective and an embedding is contractible.
mainmatter.block: Proof. Fixing b:B, we must check that the fiber of f over b is contractible. For the centre of contraction, we must construct an element of this fiber; because f is an embedding, the fiber is a proposition, so it suffices by the induction principle for propositional truncation to observe that the fiber is inhabited because f is surjective.

mainmatter.p: A function f \colon A \to B that is both surjective and an embedding is biinvertible.
mainmatter.block: Proof. As contractible maps are biinvertible, it is enough to recall that surjective embeddings are contractible.

mainmatter.p: Let f , g \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx be two dependent functions. A homotopy from f to g, writing H \colon f \sim g is given by a dependent function H \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } fx=gx. We shall write 1_{ f } \colon f \sim f for the identity homotopy on f defined by 1_{ f } x: \equiv \mathsf {refl}.
Let s \colon B \to A and r \colon A \to B be a pair of maps. A homotopy h \colon r \circ s \sim 1_{ B } exhibits s as a section of r and r as a retraction of s.

Let f \colon A \to B and g \colon B \to C be maps equipped with sections s_f \colon B \to A and s_g \colon C \to B. The composite s_f \circ s_g \colon C \to A is a section of g \circ f \colon A \to C.
mainmatter.block: Proof. The witness of sectionhood is defined using the witnesses for the two sections.

mainmatter.p: Let f \colon A \to B and g \colon B \to C be maps equipped with retractions r_f \colon B \to A and r_g \colon C \to B. The composite r_f \circ r_g \colon C \to A is a retraction of g \circ f \colon A \to C.
mainmatter.block: Proof. The witness of retractionhood is defined using the witnesses for the two retractions.

mainmatter.p: When s \colon A \to B is a section of r \colon B \to A, we say that A is a retract of B.
A biinverse to a function f \colon A \to B is defined to be a pair of a section and a retraction to f.

Let f \colon A \to B and g \colon B \to C be biinvertible maps. Then the composite g \circ f \colon A \to C is biinvertible, with sections and retractions given by composites of the component sections and retractions.

Let f \colon A \to B and g \colon B \to C be quasi-invertible maps. Then the composite g \circ f \colon A \to C is quasi-invertible, with sectionhood and retractionhood witnesses given by those of the composites of the component sections and retractions.
An equivalence from A to B is defined to be a function A \to B equipped with a biinverse.

A quasi-inverse to a function f \colon A \to B is a function g \colon B \to A equipped with homotopies \epsilon \colon f \circ g \sim 1_{ B } and \eta \colon g \circ f \sim 1_{ A }, rendering g simultaneously a section and a retraction of f.
Although no coherence laws are assumed for \eta and \epsilon, we shall refer to them respectively as the unit and counit of the quasi-inverse.
Any quasi-invertible map f \colon A \to B is biinvertible.
mainmatter.block: Proof. The quasi-inverse to f is simultaneously a section and a retraction.

mainmatter.p: An inverse to a function f \colon A \to B is defined to be a quasi-inverse equipped with an additional higher coherence \chi \colon \mathsf {ap}_{ f } \circ \eta \sim \epsilon \circ f in f \circ g \circ f \sim f relating the (a priori incoherent) unit and the counit of the quasi-inverse.
The fiber of a function f \colon A \to B at an element b:B is the type consisting of elements a \colon A equipped with identifications p \colon fa=b.

A type A is said to be contractible with centre a:A when every x:A is equipped with an identification \chi _x \colon a = x. We say that A is contractible when it comes equipped with some a:A such that A is contractible with centre a. We sometimes refer to a contractible type as a singleton.
A type A is said to be a proposition when every x:A, the type A is contractible with centre x, i.e. all elements of A are identified.
A type A is called a set when for each x,y:A the identification type x=y is a proposition.
The notions of contractible and propositional types generate an infinite hierarchy of truncation levels expressing the point after which higher identifications become trivial.
A truncation level is an integer n \geq -2.
Given a truncation level n, we define what it means for a type to be n-truncated by induction:

ul.li: a type is -2-truncated when it is contractible;
a type is -1-truncated when it is a proposition;
for n \geq 0, a type A is n-truncated when for every x,y:A the identification type x=y is n-1-truncated.

mainmatter.p: So, in particular, a 0-truncated type is precisely a set. We shall often refer to n-truncated types as n-types.

A function f \colon A \to B is called n-truncated when all of its fibers are n-truncated types. By analogy, we shall also refer to maps as contractible when their fibers are; we shall refer to maps as embeddings when their fibers are propositions.
If a type A is contractible, then it is a proposition.
mainmatter.block: Proof. As A is contractible with some centre a:A, we have for any x,y:A identifications \chi _x \colon a = x and \chi _y \colon a = y. Therefore, we identify x with y using \chi _x ^{-1} \cdot \chi _y.

mainmatter.p: If a type A is a proposition, then it is a set.
If a type A is n-truncated, then it is also n+1-truncated.
mainmatter.block:
Proof. We proceed by induction on the truncation level n, with two base cases:
If n=-2, we recall that contractible types are propositions.
If n=-1, we recall that propositions are sets.

mainmatter.p: For any type A, the identity map 1_{ A } \colon A \to A is contractible.
mainmatter.block: Proof. Immediate by identification induction.

frontmatter.title: The identity map is n-truncated for all n

mainmatter.p: For any type A, the identity map 1_{ A } \colon A \to A is n-truncated for all n.
mainmatter.block: Proof. We proceed by induction on the truncation level n: when n=-2, we recall that the identity map is contractible, and in the inductive step we apply the upward closure of n-truncation to each fiber of 1_{ A }.

mainmatter.p: If A is a retract of B and B is a proposition, then A is a proposition.
mainmatter.block: Proof. This can be seen using the cancellation laws of retractions.

frontmatter.title: Contractible types are closed under retracts

mainmatter.p: If A is a retract of B and B is contractible, then A is a contractible.
mainmatter.block: Proof. Let s \colon A \to B be a section of r \colon B \to A; if b_0 is the centre of contraction of B, then the centre of contraction of A is given by rb_0. For the rest, we use the fact that propositions are closed under retracts and contractible types are propositions.

mainmatter.p: For any type A we have a proposition \mathsf {toProp} \, {A} equipped with a unit map \eta _{ \mathsf {prop}} \colon A \to \mathsf {toProp} \, {A}; moreover, we have the following induction principle: for any family of propositions B \colon \mathsf {toProp} \, A \to \mathsf {Prop}, any function b \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } B \mathopen {} \left ( \eta _{ \mathsf {prop}} \, x \right ) \mathclose {} extends to a function b^ \sharp \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x \colon \mathsf {toProp} \, A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x \colon \mathsf {toProp} \, A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x \colon \mathsf {toProp} \, A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x \colon \mathsf {toProp} \, A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx.
When we have an element of \mathsf {toProp} \, A, we will say that A is inhabited.
A type A is called inhabited when we have an element of \mathsf {toProp} \, A. When we say there exists, we refer to inhabitation.
A function f \colon A \to B is called surjective when for any b \colon B, the fiber of f over b is inhabited.
